population: General/healthy population from 12 prospective cohort studies (primary prevention; excluded studies with prior clinical events), total 1,574,299 subjects; sample sizes per study ranged 161 to 214,284; follow-up 3.7 to 18 years; six cohorts from Mediterranean populations, others from US, northern Europe, and Europeans living in Australia.
intervention: Higher adherence to a Mediterranean diet, measured by an a priori adherence score (points for higher intake of vegetables, fruits, legumes, cereals, fish, moderate red wine; fewer points for red/processed meats and dairy), specifically a two-point increase in the score.
comparator: Lower adherence to a Mediterranean diet (lower score) within the same cohorts.
outcome: A two-point increase in Mediterranean diet adherence score was associated with: reduced overall mortality (RR 0.91, 95% CI 0.89-0.94; P<0.0001); reduced cardiovascular mortality (RR 0.91, 95% CI 0.87-0.95; P<0.0001); reduced cancer incidence/mortality (RR 0.94, 95% CI 0.92-0.96; P<0.0001); and reduced incidence of Parkinson's disease and Alzheimer's disease (RR 0.87, 95% CI 0.80-0.96; P=0.004).
clinical_bottom_line: For primary prevention counseling in the general population, greater adherence to a Mediterranean-style dietary pattern (more vegetables, fruits, legumes, cereals, fish, moderate red wine; less red/processed meat and dairy) is associated with meaningful reductions in overall mortality, cardiovascular mortality, cancer incidence/mortality, and Parkinson's/Alzheimer's incidence, supporting recommending this dietary pattern as a preventive measure.
study_quality_limitations: This is a meta-analysis of observational prospective cohort studies (not RCTs), so it is subject to residual confounding, especially given differing adjustment for confounders across studies. There was heterogeneity in how the diet score categorized food groups (e.g., grouping of vegetables/potatoes, meat/poultry, nuts/seeds, dairy) across studies. Significant heterogeneity (I2=48.8%) was initially present for overall mortality, though it decreased after excluding a study with overlapping cohort data. The score itself is subjective and limited by available data. Sensitivity analyses by country, sex, follow-up time, and study quality showed no significant influence on results. Failsafe N calculations for all outcomes exceeded Rosenthal's recommended threshold, suggesting robustness against publication bias.

population: 87,256 women (age 30-55 y at baseline) and 45,490 men (age 40-75 y at baseline) without a history of cancer, followed for up to 26 years
intervention: Higher adherence to the DASH-style diet score (and separately, Alternate Mediterranean Diet [aMed] score), assessed via dietary information collected up to 7 times during follow-up
comparator: Lower adherence to the DASH-style diet score (bottom quintile) versus higher adherence (top quintile); same comparison for aMed score
outcome: Incident colorectal cancer (1432 cases in women, 1032 cases in men); pooled RR for total colorectal cancer comparing top vs bottom quintile of DASH score was 0.80 (95% CI: 0.70, 0.91; P for trend = 0.0001); RR for colon cancer was 0.81 (95% CI: 0.69, 0.95; P for trend = 0.002); RR for rectal cancer was 0.73 (95% CI: 0.55, 0.98; P for trend = 0.31); no association observed with aMed score
clinical_bottom_line: Higher adherence to a DASH-style dietary pattern (more whole grains, fruit, vegetables, low-fat dairy; less red/processed meat, desserts, sweetened beverages) was associated with lower long-term risk of colorectal and colon cancer, supporting counseling patients toward this eating pattern for colorectal cancer risk reduction rather than the Mediterranean diet score used here, which showed no association.

population: Patients being prescribed methadone (methadone treatment patients)
intervention: QTc interval screening protocol: pretreatment electrocardiogram, follow-up ECG within 30 days and annually, additional ECG if methadone dose exceeds 100 mg/d or unexplained syncope/seizures, plus clinical history-taking and disclosure of arrhythmia risk
outcome: Risk stratification recommendations based on QTc interval: 450-500 ms warrants risk-benefit discussion and closer monitoring; >500 ms warrants considering dose reduction/discontinuation, eliminating contributing factors (e.g., hypokalemia-promoting drugs), or alternative therapy
clinical_bottom_line: Before and during methadone therapy, obtain baseline, 30-day, and annual ECGs (with additional ECGs at doses >100 mg/d or with unexplained syncope/seizures); if QTc is 450-500 ms, intensify monitoring and discuss risks/benefits, and if QTc exceeds 500 ms, consider reducing/discontinuing methadone, correcting contributing factors like hypokalemia, or switching to an alternative therapy.
study_quality_limitations: This is an expert consensus statement based on panel review of literature, national guidelines, and regulatory information rather than a clinical trial with primary outcome data.

intervention: Isocaloric test meals (vegetables, protein/chicken breast, carbohydrate/white rice) consumed in varying food intake sequences, including vegetable-first-then-meat-then-rice (V-M-R), vegetable-then-meat-and-rice together (V-MR), meat-first-then-vegetable-then-rice (M-VR), and vegetable-meat-rice consumed together (VMR)
comparator: Rice consumed first followed by vegetable and meat (R-VM)
outcome: Postprandial plasma glucose, insulin, GIP and GLP-1 responses over 3 hours; significant food intake sequence × time interaction effects (P < 0.001); glucose response attenuated with V-MR, M-VR, V-M-R, and VMR compared to R-VM; insulin iAUC(0-60) lower after V-M-R than M-VR, VMR, and R-VM; V-M-R produced higher GLP-1 release than other sequences; GIP response lower after V-MR and V-M-R than M-VR and R-MR
clinical_bottom_line: In healthy adults, eating vegetables first, then protein, then carbohydrate (rice) last blunts the postprandial glucose rise and lowers early insulin demand while increasing GLP-1 release compared to eating rice first — supporting food sequencing (vegetable-protein-carbohydrate order) as a simple bedside/dietary strategy to reduce glycemic excursions after meals.
study_quality_limitations: Randomized, controlled, crossover design in healthy adults only; no blinding status stated.

population: 32 people who use illegal opioids (PWUIO) in the New York City area who reported experiencing opioid withdrawal at least once in the past 30 days; 50% male, 46% female, 4% undisclosed gender; 40% white, 25% Black, 28% Latinx, 6% undisclosed race; mean age 40.8; recruited purposively from an ongoing longitudinal study on overdose risk management in the era of naloxone.
outcome: Qualitative thematic analysis (using the risk environment framework) of semi-structured interviews describing withdrawal experiences and their relationship to risk-involved behavior. Participants described withdrawal as ubiquitous, painful, and worsened by fentanyl (more frequent, more severe symptoms). Withdrawal was reported as a catalyst for risk behaviors including stealing, violent crime, risky sex work/panhandling, syringe sharing, using larger drug amounts, and reduced use of fentanyl test strips or safer injection practices. Ability to avoid or manage withdrawal (and thus reduce need for risk behavior) was linked to economic resources, social capital/networks, housing stability, and access to a safe place to use; drug policy/criminalization and fentanyl proliferation were described as structural constraints limiting participants' options and increasing risk exposure. Many participants reported difficulty remaining on methadone maintenance treatment (MMT) due to strict regulatory requirements.
clinical_bottom_line: Findings support prioritizing rapid, adequate elimination of withdrawal (e.g., via low-threshold, less punitive MOUD programs with induction/dosing protocols adjusted for high fentanyl-related tolerance) as a way to reduce patients' need to engage in risk-involved behaviors like syringe sharing or forgoing fentanyl test strips, since participants described withdrawal avoidance as directly driving these behaviors.
study_quality_limitations: Qualitative study limited to 32 interviews from the NYC area, where fentanyl proliferation and local drug policy/policing context may not generalize to other locations; interviewer disclosed his own lived experience with illegal opioid use and current MMT status to participants as part of a 'situated approach,' which the authors acknowledge as a methodological choice with associated issues discussed elsewhere; the authors' risk environment analysis did not identically mirror Rhodes' original typology.

population: 44,444 men from the Health Professionals Follow-up Study (predominantly white, aged 40-75 at baseline in 1986, with no history of gout before 1986), followed over 26 years
intervention: Higher adherence to the DASH dietary pattern (high intake of fruits, vegetables, nuts and legumes, low fat dairy, whole grains; low intake of sodium, sweetened beverages, and red/processed meats), assessed via food frequency questionnaires and cumulative average intake over time
comparator: Lower adherence to the DASH dietary pattern (lowest fifth of DASH score); separately, higher versus lower adherence to a Western dietary pattern (high intake of red/processed meats, sugar-sweetened beverages, sweets, desserts, French fries, refined grains)
outcome: Incident gout meeting American College of Rheumatology survey criteria; highest vs lowest fifth of DASH score had relative risk 0.68 (95% CI 0.57 to 0.80, P for trend <0.001) for incident gout; highest vs lowest fifth of Western dietary pattern score had relative risk 1.42 (95% CI 1.16 to 1.74, P for trend 0.005) for incident gout; associations were independent of age, BMI, hypertension, renal failure, diuretic use, alcohol intake, and coffee intake, and did not differ by BMI, alcohol use, or hypertension status
clinical_bottom_line: In men without prior gout, greater adherence to a DASH-style eating pattern was associated with a lower risk of developing gout, while a Western dietary pattern was associated with a higher risk, supporting recommending the DASH diet as a dietary strategy for gout prevention (particularly in patients who already have hypertension) rather than the conventional low-purine diet.
study_quality_limitations: Observational prospective cohort (not a randomized trial), so causality cannot be firmly established; dietary intake was self-reported via questionnaire, which the authors state introduces some inevitable misclassification of exposure, though they note this would likely bias results toward the null; cohort was limited to male health professionals, predominantly white, aged 40-75, limiting direct generalizability to other populations (e.g., women, African-Americans, general population).

population: Adult heroin addicts treated with methadone or buprenorphine on a daily basis at the Drug Addiction Service in Copenhagen (450 patients, approximately 52% of those eligible)
outcome: QT/QTc interval prolongation and self-reported syncope; methadone dose was associated with longer QT interval (0.140 ms/mg, p=0.002), 28% of men and 32% of women on methadone had prolonged QTc, no buprenorphine-treated subjects had QTc >0.440 s, and a 50 mg higher methadone dose was associated with 1.2 (95% CI 1.1 to 1.4) times higher odds of syncope; no association found between buprenorphine and QTc
clinical_bottom_line: In heroin-dependent patients, higher methadone doses are associated with dose-dependent QT prolongation and increased odds of syncope, whereas buprenorphine was not associated with QTc prolongation, supporting closer ECG/QTc monitoring or consideration of buprenorphine in patients at risk for QT-related events.
study_quality_limitations: Cross-sectional design; only approximately 52% of eligible patients were included

population: 4336 men and 6435 women recruited through health food shops, vegetarian societies, and magazines in the United Kingdom, described as vegetarians and other health conscious people, followed for a mean of 16.8 years
intervention: Daily consumption of fresh fruit (also assessed: vegetarianism and daily consumption of wholemeal bread, bran cereals, nuts or dried fruit, and raw salad)
comparator: Less than daily consumption of fresh fruit (or non-vegetarian status/less than daily consumption of the other foods)
outcome: All cause mortality and mortality from ischaemic heart disease, cerebrovascular disease, all malignant neoplasms, lung cancer, colorectal cancer, and breast cancer; daily fresh fruit consumption was associated with reduced mortality from ischaemic heart disease (rate ratio 0.76, 95% CI 0.60-0.97), cerebrovascular disease (0.68, 0.47-0.98), and all causes combined (0.79, 0.70-0.90)
clinical_bottom_line: In this health-conscious cohort, daily fresh fruit intake was associated with lower all-cause, ischaemic heart disease, and cerebrovascular mortality, supporting counseling patients toward daily fresh fruit consumption as part of dietary advice, though this observational association should not be interpreted as proof of a causal treatment effect.
study_quality_limitations: Observational study design; results adjusted for smoking only as stated

population: Hospitalized patients with opioid use disorder seen by an inpatient addiction consult team at an academic medical center, newly initiated on methadone between 2016 and 2022 (n=112)
intervention: Rapid inpatient methadone induction with dose escalation (mean initial dose 32 mg, mean maximum dose 76.8 mg, mean 5.6 days from initial to peak dose)
comparator: Comparison within cohort between patients with or without sedation, and between patients ending at doses of 100 mg or higher versus lower doses
outcome: 30% of patients experienced a safety event, most commonly sedation; only 4 safety events were deemed probably or definitely related to methadone; no significant difference in starting dose between patients with or without sedation; ending dose of 100 mg or higher was associated with higher likelihood of any possibly related event (47.8% vs 12.4%, P < .001); 76% were connected to an opioid treatment program before discharge
clinical_bottom_line: Rapid inpatient methadone titration to a mean peak dose of ~77 mg over about 5.6 days was infrequently associated with methadone-related safety events, supporting faster in-hospital dose escalation than typical outpatient guidance, though clinicians should be more cautious about safety events as doses reach 100 mg or higher.
study_quality_limitations: Retrospective, observational analysis; only a single academic medical center setting described

population: Participants from cohort studies examining DASH-style diet adherence in relation to cardiovascular diseases, CHD, stroke, and heart failure; cohort studies including participants with specific CVD risk factors like diabetes mellitus, metabolic syndrome, obesity, or hypertension were excluded
intervention: Higher adherence to/imitation of a DASH-style diet
comparator: Lower adherence to a DASH-style diet
outcome: Incidence of cardiovascular diseases (CVDs), coronary heart disease (CHD), stroke, and heart failure (HF); DASH diet adherence associated with reduced risk of CVDs (RR 0.80), CHD (RR 0.79), stroke (RR 0.81), and HF (RR 0.71), with a linear negative association between DASH concordance and CVD risk
clinical_bottom_line: Observational evidence supports recommending greater adherence to a DASH-style diet as a dietary strategy associated with lower long-term risk of cardiovascular disease, CHD, stroke, and heart failure, supporting its use in counseling patients on cardiovascular risk reduction.
study_quality_limitations: Based on observational prospective cohort studies (not randomized trials); only 6 studies met inclusion criteria for this systematic review and meta-analysis

population: 110,799 individuals aged 40 to 75 years from the UK Biobank cohort, free of CVD or cancer, assessed between 2009 and 2012 and followed up to 2021
intervention: Higher adherence to a Mediterranean lifestyle, measured by the MEDLIFE index (Mediterranean food consumption, Mediterranean dietary habits, and physical activity/rest/social habits/conviviality)
comparator: Lower adherence to the Mediterranean lifestyle (first quartile of MEDLIFE index) compared with increasing quartiles
outcome: All-cause mortality, cancer mortality, and CVD mortality; higher MEDLIFE quartiles showed progressively lower HRs for all-cause mortality (0.89, 0.81, 0.71) and cancer mortality (0.90, 0.83, 0.72), both P-trend<.001; all MEDLIFE blocks were independently associated with lower all-cause and cancer death, and block 3 (physical activity, rest, social habits, conviviality) was associated with lower CVD mortality
clinical_bottom_line: In middle-aged and older adults without CVD or cancer, greater adherence to a Mediterranean-style lifestyle (diet plus physical activity, rest, and social habits) is associated with a dose-dependent reduction in all-cause and cancer mortality, supporting counseling patients toward broader Mediterranean lifestyle adoption (not diet alone) as part of routine preventive advice.

population: 459 adults with systolic blood pressure less than 160 mm Hg and diastolic blood pressure of 80 to 95 mm Hg
intervention: Combination diet rich in fruits, vegetables, and low-fat dairy products with reduced saturated and total fat (also a fruits-and-vegetables-only diet arm), fed for eight weeks after a three-week control diet run-in, with sodium intake and body weight held constant
comparator: Control diet low in fruits, vegetables, and dairy products, with fat content typical of the average US diet
outcome: Combination diet reduced systolic blood pressure by 5.5 mm Hg and diastolic blood pressure by 3.0 mm Hg more than control (P<0.001 for each); fruits-and-vegetables diet reduced systolic by 2.8 mm Hg more (P<0.001) and diastolic by 1.1 mm Hg more (P=0.07) than control. Among subjects with hypertension, combination diet reduced systolic by 11.4 mm Hg and diastolic by 5.5 mm Hg more than control (P<0.001 each); among those without hypertension, reductions were 3.5 mm Hg (P<0.001) and 2.1 mm Hg (P=0.003).
clinical_bottom_line: In patients with blood pressures in this range, adopting a diet rich in fruits, vegetables, and low-fat dairy with reduced saturated and total fat (with sodium and weight held constant) can substantially lower blood pressure, with the largest effect seen in those already hypertensive, supporting dietary counseling as an adjunct approach to lowering blood pressure.
study_quality_limitations: Randomized controlled trial with a controlled feeding design in which sodium intake and body weight were maintained constant, which may limit generalizability to free-living conditions with usual sodium intake and weight variation; this limitation is implied by the constant-sodium/weight design described but not explicitly labeled a limitation by the authors.

population: Three patients undergoing methadone treatment for opioid use disorder (OUD)
intervention: Methadone treatment, with management strategies including methadone dose reduction, transition to buprenorphine, and cardiac therapies such as antiarrhythmics and implantable cardioverter-defibrillators (ICDs)
outcome: Development of significant QT prolongation and Torsades de Pointes (TdP), with review of patient-specific risk factors, management strategies used, and post-hospitalization outcomes and follow-up

population: Adults from the general population (excluding those with chronic disease), drawn from 185 prospective studies (~135 million person-years) and 58 clinical trials with 4635 adult participants
intervention: Higher dietary fibre intake and higher whole grain intake (and lower glycaemic index/load) as markers of carbohydrate quality
comparator: Lower dietary fibre intake, lower whole grain intake, or higher glycaemic index/load
outcome: All-cause and cardiovascular mortality, coronary heart disease, stroke incidence and mortality, type 2 diabetes, colorectal cancer incidence, bodyweight, systolic blood pressure, and total cholesterol; observational data showed 15-30% lower risk of these outcomes with highest vs lowest fibre consumption, with greatest risk reduction at daily fibre intake of 25-29 g, and clinical trials showed lower bodyweight, systolic blood pressure, and total cholesterol with higher fibre intake; similar findings were seen for whole grains, with smaller or no risk reductions for glycaemic index/load
clinical_bottom_line: For inpatients being counseled on diet, encouraging dietary fibre intake in the range of 25-29 g/day (and replacing refined grains with whole grains) is supported by evidence of reduced risk of major non-communicable diseases and improved bodyweight, blood pressure, and cholesterol, whereas targeting glycaemic index/load shows weaker evidence and is not supported as a priority intervention.
study_quality_limitations: Certainty of evidence was graded as moderate for dietary fibre, low to moderate for whole grains, and low to very low for glycaemic index and glycaemic load; findings are limited to risk reduction in the general population rather than those with chronic disease; data on other dietary exposures are scarce.

clinical_bottom_line: Automated ECG QT readings should not be trusted; clinicians should manually measure and heart-rate-correct the QT interval themselves, especially when patients are on medications that can prolong QT, to properly assess torsades de pointes risk.
study_quality_limitations: This is a review article, not an original study; no correction formula has been shown to be superior to others.

clinical_bottom_line: Residents should be aware that abrupt cessation of medetomidine (an increasingly common fentanyl adulterant that has replaced xylazine in some areas) can cause a severe autonomic withdrawal syndrome with severe hypertension, tachycardia, vomiting, and tremors, often requiring hospital admission and critical care; this should be on the differential when triaging opioid users with unexplained severe autonomic instability, though the text does not report a specific treatment or management protocol.
study_quality_limitations: The text notes that drug checking and testing of biospecimens for medetomidine remains limited.

population: Patients with opioid use disorder restarting methadone at a hospital-based opioid treatment program (OTP) in Denver, Colorado after missing 4 or more days of dosing (preimplementation period: 202 patients, 464 restart episodes; postimplementation period: 195 patients, 489 restart episodes)
intervention: A novel, individualized/patient-centered methadone restart protocol implemented in 2022 that assessed interim opioid use via patient report, urine toxicology, and/or signs of intoxication/withdrawal, allowing smaller dose reductions for patients with maintained tolerance
comparator: Prior standard restart protocol (preimplementation period, 2021) that reduced methadone dose by 50% after missing 4-6 days and restarted at 30 mg or less after missing 7 or more days
outcome: Primary: ED visits within 7 days of restart and all-cause mortality within 7 and 90 days; ED visits for opioid withdrawal/overdose within 7 days. Secondary: 90-day treatment retention. Process outcome: percentage change in methadone dose from pre-gap to restart. Results: restart dose reduction was 32.8% preimplementation vs 3.4% postimplementation (P<.001); ED visits within 7 days were lower postimplementation (9.5% vs 6.1%; adjusted RR 0.61, 95% CI 0.37-0.98); withdrawal/overdose ED visits and overdose deaths were rare in both periods with no overdose deaths postimplementation; 90-day retention was similar between periods (52.2% vs 51.7%; adjusted RR 0.88, 95% CI 0.73-1.05).
clinical_bottom_line: An individualized restart protocol allowing much smaller methadone dose reductions (accounting for interim opioid tolerance) rather than reflexive halving/very low restart doses was associated with fewer 7-day ED visits and no increase in overdose or mortality, without change in 90-day retention, suggesting clinicians can consider more clinically tailored, less drastic dose reductions at restart rather than uniform steep cuts.
study_quality_limitations: Observational, retrospective pre-post design without an external control group, so outcome differences may not be solely attributable to the new protocol; prior restart doses were already higher than typically recommended, muting the apparent effect; rare safety events (nonfatal overdose, mortality) mean the study is likely underpowered to detect small but clinically meaningful safety differences; nonfatal overdoses managed in the community without ED transport may be underascertained; ED data came from only a single health system, missing visits elsewhere; ongoing illicit substance use after restart was not systematically assessed; patient survey used a convenience sample potentially biased toward more extreme opinions.

population: Directors of hospital-based addiction consult services (ACS) affiliated with ACGME-accredited addiction medicine and addiction psychiatry fellowship programs across the US (50 states, Washington DC, Puerto Rico), surveyed October 2023 to April 2024.
intervention: Self-reported use of novel/adapted methadone initiation practices (e.g., rapid methadone initiation, adjunctive medications for withdrawal) and buprenorphine initiation practices (low-dose, high-dose, traditional, and buprenorphine rescue) in the hospital setting.
comparator: Standard/traditional methadone and buprenorphine initiation practices as defined by SAMHSA 2021 guidance and ASAM Clinical Considerations.
outcome: Proportion of ACS directors offering methadone and buprenorphine initiation, use of rapid methadone initiation (87.0% of those initiating methadone) and adjunctive withdrawal medications (95.7%), and use of buprenorphine initiation methods (low-dose most common at 92.9%, followed by traditional 87.7%, high-dose 75.4%, and buprenorphine rescue 35.1%), including practice selection across 7 clinical scenarios where low-dose initiation was preferred except in patients with significant withdrawal 2 days after last fentanyl use, where traditional or high-dose initiation was preferred.
clinical_bottom_line: Among academic hospital addiction consult services, rapid methadone initiation with adjunctive full-agonist opioids/other medications for breakthrough withdrawal, and low-dose buprenorphine initiation (overlapping with full agonists), are now the predominant practices in response to high-potency synthetic opioids; however, for patients in significant withdrawal 2 days after last fentanyl use, traditional or high-dose buprenorphine initiation was more commonly selected.
study_quality_limitations: Survey study limited to academic, predominantly urban ACS, so results may not generalize to other settings; relies on self-reported current practice which may differ from actual practice; captured only published/described practices and may have missed unpublished approaches; respondents may have interpreted provided definitions (e.g., rapid methadone initiation, low-dose buprenorphine initiation) differently despite definitions being provided.

population: A 55-year-old man with well-controlled Crohn disease, treated with azathioprine, presenting with spontaneous extensive hematomas of both lower limbs, hyperregenerative anemia, and a severely restricted diet (5 foods, no fruit or vegetable intake for over a year)
intervention: Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) supplementation (1000 mg/day for 5 days, then 500 mg twice daily for 1 month) plus dietary modifications
outcome: Rapid improvement in hemoglobin levels and progressive resolution of the hematomas; azathioprine was later successfully reintroduced with no adverse events, and the patient remained asymptomatic at follow-up
clinical_bottom_line: In patients with restrictive diets or malabsorptive conditions presenting with unexplained hematomas/bleeding and a negative standard coagulation workup, consider scurvy as a reversible cause and evaluate diet history and skin findings (e.g., follicular purpura) before attributing bleeding solely to drug effects like azathioprine.
study_quality_limitations: This is a single case report; vitamin C level was measured 5 days after dietary correction began and was only at the lower limit of normal, which the authors note may reflect rapid improvement after supplementation rather than the true initial deficiency level.

population: Subjects from 21 cohort studies (1,368,736 participants) and 12 case-control studies (62,725 participants) included in a meta-analysis of Mediterranean diet adherence and cancer risk
intervention: Highest adherence to a Mediterranean diet (MD)
comparator: Lower/lowest adherence category to Mediterranean diet, as categorized within the included studies
outcome: Overall cancer mortality/incidence, and risk of colorectal, prostate, aerodigestive, breast, gastric, and pancreatic cancers
clinical_bottom_line: In observational data, higher adherence to a Mediterranean diet is associated with modestly lower overall cancer mortality/incidence and lower colorectal, prostate, and aerodigestive cancer risk, supporting counseling patients toward Mediterranean-style eating as a general risk-reduction measure rather than a treatment for existing cancer.
study_quality_limitations: Based on observational cohort and case-control studies rather than randomized trials; substantial heterogeneity noted for several outcomes (I2 up to 83%); Egger regression tests showed limited evidence of publication bias.

population: Adults at increased cardiovascular risk, including those with two or more established cardiovascular risk factors (e.g., hypertension, dyslipidaemia, obesity, diabetes) or established cardiovascular disease (coronary artery disease, myocardial infarction, stroke, peripheral artery disease); 40 randomised controlled trials (n=35,548), 9 primary prevention and 31 secondary prevention trials, median follow-up 3 years (range 0.75-17 years).
intervention: Structured dietary programmes, classified by named diet category: low fat, very low fat, modified fat, combined low fat and low sodium, Mediterranean, Ornish, and Pritikin; programmes could include cointerventions such as exercise, psychosocial/behavioural support, smoking cessation, or drug treatment.
comparator: Minimal intervention (no dietary advice, usual care, infrequent/brief dietary advice, or educational sessions on non-dietary matters) or other dietary programmes (head-to-head comparisons via network meta-analysis).
outcome: All cause mortality, cardiovascular mortality, stroke, non-fatal myocardial infarction, and unplanned cardiovascular interventions at last reported follow-up. Mediterranean dietary programmes were superior to minimal intervention for all cause mortality (OR 0.72), cardiovascular mortality (OR 0.55), stroke (OR 0.65), and non-fatal MI (OR 0.48), based on moderate certainty evidence. Low fat dietary programmes were superior to minimal intervention for all cause mortality (OR 0.84), non-fatal MI (OR 0.77), and unplanned cardiovascular interventions (OR 0.57, low certainty), based on moderate certainty evidence for mortality and MI. No convincing evidence that Mediterranean was superior to low fat for mortality or non-fatal MI, though moderate certainty evidence suggested possible superiority for stroke. Very low fat, combined low fat and low sodium, modified fat, Ornish, and Pritikin diets showed little or no benefit over minimal intervention for most outcomes (mostly low/very low certainty), except combined low fat and low sodium showed possible benefit for stroke in high-risk patients.
clinical_bottom_line: For inpatients with elevated cardiovascular risk, recommending a Mediterranean or low fat structured dietary programme (rather than minimal/usual dietary advice) is supported by moderate certainty evidence for reducing all cause mortality and non-fatal myocardial infarction, with Mediterranean diets additionally supported for stroke reduction; there is no convincing evidence to prefer Mediterranean over low fat diets for mortality or MI, and very low fat, modified fat, Ornish, Pritikin, or combined low fat/low sodium diets should not be prioritized given lack of convincing benefit.
study_quality_limitations: 13 of 40 trials were judged low overall risk of bias and 27 at high risk of bias. Certainty of evidence was rated down for indirectness in low fat and Mediterranean comparisons due to varying cointerventions (smoking cessation, drug treatment) in trials versus minimal intervention. Sensitivity analyses excluding trials with smoking cessation or drug treatment cointerventions showed loss of statistical significance for low fat dietary programmes on all cause mortality, non-fatal MI, and unplanned cardiovascular interventions, while Mediterranean diet findings remained significant (though based on higher risk of bias trials). Adherence to dietary programmes was not systematically factored into analysis due to lack of reporting across trials. An earlier version (v1) of the Cochrane risk of bias tool was used rather than the latest version. Minimal intervention control arms were heterogeneous across trials. The review did not include one recent trial (CORDIOPREV) published after the data analysis was completed. Two studies were excluded in sensitivity analyses due to concerns about data integrity.
